data.title: CXSE: China Ex-SOE Approach
data.description:
WisdomTree China ex-State-Owned Enterprises Fund Outlook

Chinese equities trade at a substantial discount to emerging markets following their sharp correction from the highs of 2021. Investors who target China now can purchase shares near the 2020 Covid lows.

Strategically targeting China in 2026 looks like a favorable strategy for emerging market investors, as this market is uniquely positioned relative to other major Asian stock markets like Taiwan and India, which trade at a massive premium to China.

The WisdomTree China ex-State-Owned Enterprises Fund (CXSE) has sold off strongly from its 2021 peak. CXSE looks like a safer vehicle at the moment because it targets non-SOE companies and invests in some of the new, higher-growth industries in China.

WisdomTree carefully selects non-SOEs that have stronger financial indicators and are strategically positioned in higher-growth industries. While other China-focused ETFs may have lower valuations, they have more exposure to SOEs in lower-valued industries like financials, energy, materials, and utilities.

CXSE definitely looks like a better ETF to own during a bull market. While emerging markets could be due for pullbacks in the coming quarters, I think CXSE stands out because of its unique company and industry approach. I also think that China should not trade at a discount to emerging markets. While returns will likely be more moderate this year, following China's circa 35% rally in 2025, CXSE still looks positioned to deliver moderate returns amid EM headwinds. CXSE looks like a much better vehicle for China bulls to own.

WisdomTree China ex-State-Owned Enterprises Overview

The WisdomTree China ex-State-Owned Enterprises Fund's main objective is to provide exposure to non-SOE stocks in China, focusing more on key growth sectors like IT.

One of the main benefits of this approach has been superior performance, as many non-SOE stocks also have higher ROEs and ROAs and have outperformed SOEs in the long run.

CXSE is consequently an excellent vehicle to own, especially during market bull runs, when it has been able to trounce other China-focused ETFs. It outperformed major Chinese ETFs between November 2016 and Q1 2021, including tech ETFs like the KraneShares CSI China Internet ETF (KWEB).

The WisdomTree China ex-State-Owned Enterprises Fund charges a modest 0.32% management fee and primarily targets large-cap non-SOE companies in China. It has delivered steady returns of around 5% since its inception and most notably performed very well following some of the shorter bull markets in the market during the past decade.

One of the main pretexts for the slightly higher valuation of CXSE is that it targets some of the high-growth "new economy" sectors like communications services, healthcare, and information technology. However, its outperformance of other tech ETFs during market bull runs also displays how corporate governance is another likely component of its performance.

China's state-owned sector has been shrinking in terms of its stock market concentration, as private companies now account for around 40% of the market, up from 33.5% in the middle of 2024.

While sectors like energy, utilities, and financials are heavily dominated by SOEs, other high-growth themes are currently dominated by non-SOEs.

The WisdomTree China ex-State-Owned Enterprises Fund makes concentrated bets on some of the leading large-cap stocks in the market, including giants like Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) and Tencent Holdings Limited (TCEHY). CXSE is still heavily diversified, as it invests in over 260 companies, and its top 10 holdings only account for around 39% of its assets.

In my view, the divergence in performance is not purely based on the differences in corporate governance/management quality but also because of the fund's industry and large-cap approach. While this can be very positive during bull runs, it is still worth noting that this isn't a very defensive ETF, as it invests in many dominant large-cap names that are also present in emerging market ETFs. CXSE will still be vulnerable to short-term selling pressure this year, a relevant consideration given that inflation is going to pick up in emerging markets in 2026.

Based on historical price movements, CXSE may be slightly more vulnerable to drawdowns during poor economic cycles. However, this appears like a reasonable risk given CXSE's outperformance history during bull runs.

While geopolitical tensions have reached a new high, the broader emerging market class still has a valuation appeal relative to developed markets, as the historical discount of the market has been very strong in recent years. China stands out in this market as the MSCI Emerging Market Index's largest constituent, which still trades at a discount to the broader index.

For long-term investors, equities in China make a lot of sense now. Although the WisdomTree China ex-State-Owned Enterprises Fund does trade at a premium to the MSCI China Index (18.5x estimated earnings), this premium is justified because of its stronger exposure to consumer and tech themes.

Index PE PB
MSCI China Index 14.36x 1.59x
MSCI China Tech 100 Index 21.49x 2.43x
MSCI China Consumer Discretionary Index 19.24x 2.27x
MSCI China Consumer Staples Index 18.34x 4.36x

Source: MSCI

China Economy and Stock Market Outlook

China's economic growth is poised to reach a new low in 2026. Based on the latest projections from the World Bank and IMF, China's economy will only grow by 4.4-4.5% in 2026. While energy prices may slam Asian countries in 2026, China benefits from its relatively lower dependence on oil from Iran and the Strait of Hormuz. However, growth projections will likely be lowered slightly later this year, and developments in the energy markets will likely harm consumer sentiment, which has been a bit vulnerable recently.

China will likely have to rely on consumption-driven growth in the coming years, which is a challenge because of the lower consumption rate as a percentage of GDP and higher household savings rate amid economic uncertainty.

China's export growth has been off to a strong start during the first two months of this year. China's PMI recently rose to a 12-month high of around 52 last February.

China Manufacturing PMI

Moving forward, growth will most likely be driven more by China's success in new technological developments and its consumption market. This fact, coupled with its strong export diversification over the past decade, is one reason why the market still performed well in 2025.

This transformation should help support the equity market, particularly in key sectors like technology and consumer discretionary/staples. Franklin Templeton projects that the earnings growth in the market will be very strong, especially in key sectors like the technology and consumer discretionary sectors. Other themes, like industrials, will likely have slower earnings growth.

Overall, investing in Chinese large-caps does carry a lot of risks, especially since geopolitical tensions will hit all emerging stock markets this year, and China is a large constituent in many EM funds. However, China does look very appealing on a long-term basis, and those who are willing to hold through 2026 could be rewarded if shares bounce back to previous highs.

Shares of CXSE are only up around 13% from the lows of Covid in April 2020 and previously moved much higher through 2021.

CXSE has been slightly more resilient relative to other dominant Asian markets like Japan and India in the past month, and Q2 2026 could be a good time to take advantage of any further dips in the Chinese stock market.

I think that CXSE looks like a good buy at the moment, based on historical pricing and its favorable earnings growth projections, although it will likely not top its 2025 performance. For long-term investors who want to maintain exposure to emerging markets, China looks like a solid bet now.

data.title: AMD: Private Credit Crunch Is Good
data.description:
Co-authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

Shares of GPU chip giant Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) are unfortunately down almost 1% over the past month and 8% year-to-date. While shares have come under pressure with the war in Iran and Wall Street's concerns over a private credit bubble, I am reiterating my strong buy stance on AMD. In my opinion, AMD’s diverse portfolio (CPUs, GPUs, FPGAs) means they are in an excellent spot as the private credit bubble begins to show signs of deflating.

Over the last month, it's become clear that Wall Street’s $1.8 trillion private credit sector is showing signs of strain. Private credit has been one of the biggest sources of financing capital for hyperscalers, or the large data center companies that have been the driver of demand for AI-focused GPUs like the ones AMD makes.

On the surface, this feels like a net negative for the GPU maker. After all, if demand is shrinking, wouldn’t revenues drop (or at least growth slow as well)?

Normally, the answer would be yes, but AMD (through their product lineup) actually has a really strong case for why the answer could be no.

In short, AMD’s combination of GPUs, best-in-class CPUs, and edge computing offerings make them well equipped to be the ‘budget’ play for hyperscalers that are trying to scale the next generation of inference compute clusters. Inference compute clusters have been shown to be immensely profitable on a gross margin basis. This means this business model is sustainable. I expect as credit tightens, the industry will gravitate towards sustainable forms of GPU spending.

With this, I am reinforcing my strong buy stance on AMD.

Why I’m Doing Follow-Up Coverage

During my last piece of research on AMD titled “Inference Is Hitting An Inflection Point,” I argued that AMD’s AI momentum was undervalued.

Since then, we have seen what I believe to be a sea change in the way data center and AI GPU demand is being calculated. In essence, one of the biggest drivers of the huge demand for GPUs (mainly for Nvidia (NVDA)) has been the issuance of private credit (or debt) by major private credit funds in the US to finance the construction of data centers by the nation’s major hyperscalers. In my opinion, many of these data center projects do not make economic sense because the GPU depreciation timelines for these mainly Nvidia GPUs are too long. This means this could overstate short-term accounting profits.

Famed short seller Michael Burry has the same thesis. I highly recommend you check it out.

So, at this point, you might be asking yourself, if GPUs are not economically a sound investment, doesn’t this hurt AMD?

On the surface, it could, until you look at how I think the market is going to respond. When you dig deeper and look at the slowing pace of LLM advancements, you can see that many hyperscalers will need to pivot toward inference compute (which is both cheaper to operate and profitable on a per-token basis).

For those who follow the development of LLMs closely, what I mean by this statement is that many foundation labs are focusing less on increasing the reasoning capabilities of their LLMs and more on building agentic tools around these LLMs. Research studies have indicated the pace is slowing.

This is when AMD will have its moment to shine. Both the market dynamics and the slowing pace of LLM developments are forcing this. This moment is just around the corner. This is why I am doing follow-up coverage.

Private Credit Bubble Is Good

To understand why the private credit bubble should end up being a net positive for AMD, we have to look at why this will likely be a net negative for competitors like Nvidia.

For those who want a recap, private credit powers the current demand for AI GPUs by financing the hyperscalers (think companies like CoreWeave) that borrow money to buy GPUs from the GPU giants (mainly Nvidia right now based on current market share).

The issue now is that private credit default rates are rising (with some estimating that the default rates are now over 9%). I believe we could soon see a tightening of lending for private credit-financed projects. Many data centers fit into this category. We actually are seeing this already. Some of the data center projects that hyperscaler CoreWeave has wanted to launch have been stalled. Why? Their private credit backer (Blue Owl) has struggled to raise the capital necessary.

With this, hyperscalers and major foundation model companies are already facing an alleged shortage of GPU capacity to grow their business. One part of this will increasingly be that many hyperscalers can’t finance new projects.

The downstream effect of this is going to be a further pivot from larger and larger pre-training clusters and a move towards post-training inference. As I have outlined before, AMD is one of the market leaders here in cost-effective inference computing.

Research from independent journalists has shown that most major foundation model companies are profitable on a gross margin basis to produce tokens for the purpose of post-training inference. As model growth reaches a plateau, I think this is going to be a huge place that LLM model companies invest in going forward. AMD is a clear winner of this.

So while the overall GPU race may be a bubble, what comes next is an industry way more focused on the costs to provide their services (tokens). AMD is squarely in the center of this total solution opportunity. I think this is a big reason OpenAI announced their partnership with AMD last fall.

Valuation

While the stock has gone sideways since October, AMD’s forward PEG ratio signals upside, and the company’s growth-adjusted valuation remains attractive, in my opinion. AMD has a forward Non-GAAP PEG ratio of 0.70, which is well below the sector median of 1.27. This is a roughly 44% discount. To me, this discount suggests the market isn’t fully pricing in AMD’s acceleration as foundation model companies pivot to focus on profitable tokens vs. diminishing returns on pre-training clusters.

If AMD were to be re-rated to the sector median forward Non-GAAP PEG of 1.27, its shares would have strong upside potential. Applying the sector median PEG to AMD implies greater than 81% upside to the current share price. In other words, shares could trade closer to $350 (vs. ~$196 today) if the market rewarded AMD with peer-level growth multiples.

The reason I am using the PEG ratio here (despite the volatility that comes with semiconductor earnings) is because I think it signals what I think the market is expecting. A PEG ratio under 1 tells us that the market is discounting the viability of the earnings growth that AMD is expecting.

However, and while I am bearish on their cross-town rival Nvidia, if we look at their historical PEG ratio going into the ChatGPT boom in late 2022, we can see it had a similar setup. In 2022, their PEG ratio was 0.5, meaning Wall Street then also discounted their earnings growth potential. I think the same case is here now.

In terms of 2026 consensus EPS, AMD is at ~29x forward earnings at their current price. I believe that this is a conservative multiple given 40%+ EPS CAGR.

In essence, I think AMD has more growth potential from here on a YoY percent basis than its crosstown rival Nvidia.

Bear Thesis

3 months into 2026, I believe AMD is on a strong path to dominate in a world where the cost of the data center is now a serious cost component.

The biggest risk for this is that Nvidia continues to attempt to box AMD out of key deal opportunities with their CUDA ecosystem. For, if money was no object and the sole set of decisions used to decide which GPUs to purchase was the CUDA ecosystem (for developer continuity), AMD would be at a disadvantage. This is clear.

What is clear is that AMD recognizes this and is working with customers to make the jump from Nvidia to AMD far more economical. The biggest way they have incentivized customers is their warrant structure they locked in with OpenAI last fall.

While this structure was clearly dilutive to shareholders, it was a necessary deal to help push AMD further into the data center GPU market. Already, I think we are starting to see the downstream effects of helping some of the largest hyperscalers integrate AMD further into the tech stack:

Gigawatt deals that AMD has done (like those with OpenAI (OPENAI) and Meta (META)) are likely, but it's unlikely they will include warrants.

In essence, in meetings with Wall Street, AMD is signaling that large deals are in their pipeline. These deals will not need warrants to incentivize the customers.

AMD knows that by getting some of the biggest companies in the hyperscaler space to adopt their tech, it will be easier for CTOs at other firms to follow. The deals with Meta and OpenAI help them do that. I believe we have reached the turning point to break the CUDA monopoly and allow AMD to be the cost-conscious option for hyperscalers in this more cost-constrained era we’re entering.

Bottom Line

While shares have gone sideways for the last 6 months after the deal with OpenAI was announced, I’m staying bullish on AMD.

AMD is in a unique position as the brunt of computing needs related to AI shifts and the private credit bubble reaches its later stages. As foundation model companies look to focus their product lines and cut costs to focus on profitable inference-based tokens, the company has a deep product lineup that allows them to offer strong inference GPU clusters, along with CPUs that will perform well for AI agents.

AI agents have potential in the right settings with the models as is. They just need to be implemented. This is what the foundation model companies are working on now. When they do this, they’ll need to reimagine their entire tech stack to account for inference tokens that are far more economical. AMD has the answer.

With this, shares remain a strong buy. I believe the company is reaching a turning point.

data.title: Alibaba: Enormous Price For A Tough Turnaround
data.description:
Introduction

I am a long-term Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) follower, even though I have never invested in the company myself. I have covered this stock here on Seeking Alpha only twice, both times giving it a Hold rating and saying I wouldn't have bought Alibaba stock for two reasons. Since then, the stock has fallen significantly (by 30% and 17%), and today's drop is especially dramatic following the company's earnings miss.

The first reason I am staying away from this stock is that I long ago noticed how Alibaba is an exact copy of the Chinese economy. Given the geopolitical situation, I am not ready to put my money on China, even though they are standing their ground pretty successfully. But still, I have my own views on this whole political landscape, including the ongoing war in the Middle East, which, in my opinion, was directed only at China, if we are talking about the U.S. involvement. Not to turn this article into something it shouldn't be, here is my quote from my previous piece:

I see Alibaba not just as a technology company but as a proxy for the Chinese economy itself. Where the Chinese macro outlook goes, Alibaba inevitably follows, often with amplified volatility. However, there is one distinct differentiator that separates Alibaba from the broader Hang Seng index right now, and that is its aggressive pivot toward Artificial Intelligence.

And it is true: I think the company's AI efforts may help make Alibaba way more attractive, but that will happen when it stops bleeding money building out its AI capacity. And this is the second reason I wouldn't be buying Alibaba right now. While their Cloud business is, in my opinion, a great diversification and investment, their Qwen is just another large language model, or LLM, with no clear path to monetization, which burns through the company's cash. By the way, I am not skeptical of Qwen as a model, but rather of LLMs, Chinese or not, as a business decision for all the companies investing heavily in this.

After you see where I'm coming from, let's take a closer look at the earnings, valuation, and the market's reaction. If at some point in this article, you feel you disagree with my view, that's entirely fine. I'm here not to convince anyone; I'm just sharing my opinion.

December Quarter 2025 Results

Just as I opened the earnings announcement, I stumbled upon an interesting quote from the CEO, Eddie Wu, which, in my opinion, shows everything important we need to know about Alibaba right now. The first sentence immediately jumps into investments the company made, followed by AI and Cloud. This isn't surprising, as while AI, or rather cloud, will most likely be the future of this company, it is eating a lot of money out of its pockets right now. Together with that, 36% growth in the cloud segment of the business, which is way more impressive than the overall revenue growth of only 2% year-over-year, is still not as significant as the market would have hoped.

This quarter, Alibaba maintained strong investments across our core pillars of AI and consumption. AI is and will continue to be one of our primary growth engines. Our Cloud Intelligence Group’s revenue is up 36% with AI-related product revenue delivering triple-digit growth for the tenth consecutive quarter. Our Model-as-a-Service (MAAS) platform is showing strong growth, emerging as a new engine driving cloud business growth. On the consumer side, we have integrated use cases across our consumer ecosystem into Qwen app, which generated significant new users and transactions. Qwen’s consumer interface surpassed 300 million monthly active users, as AI agents perform real-world task execution at scale. Looking ahead, we are well-positioned to drive growth on both enterprise AI and consumer AI fronts, powered by our fullstack AI capabilities spanning foundation models, cloud infrastructure, and proprietary chips, alongside deep integration with our broader ecosystem.

This growth of 36% is slower than that for the U.S. peers, excluding Amazon (AMZN), which is the oldest and the biggest cloud provider of all. But comparing Alibaba to Microsoft (MSFT) and Alphabet (GOOG), we would see that the difference is still enormous. Microsoft just showed a revenue increase of 39% in Azure and other cloud services, while Alphabet's Cloud revenue surged 47.8% YoY. While it is still a lot in relative metrics, don't forget that the cloud segments of these companies are very different in size: BABA with $6.2 billion, MSFT Azure with ~$35 billion, and Alphabet with ~$18 billion. This is the reason why the market is disappointed with what looks like a good number—investors have begun realizing that the huge CapEx might not translate into huge returns.

For the quarter ended December 31, 2025, revenue from Cloud Intelligence Group was RMB43,284 million (US$6,190 million).

The company argues that Qwen would be a bridge for additional cloud customers and says their MAAS platform is growing rapidly, but once again, it doesn't show in numbers. Qwen is growing, reaching 300 million MAU, but in my opinion, this is also not the growth everyone expected to see. Comparing this to 900+ monthly active users of ChatGPT or 750+ monthly active users of Gemini, given that Alphabet's overall AI services already have over 2 billion users, the results of an Asian powerhouse could be way better.

Just so you would understand, it was the good news from Alibaba. Everything else is way less positive. Looking at the numbers, BABA looks like a company that undergoes a heavy transition phase. Their top number held up at RMB284,843 million (US$40,732 million), which was an increase of 2% year-over-year, but the bottom line tanked as a result of the heavy investing cycle, on which the management puts so many hopes. Their income from operations of RMB10,645 million (US$1,522 million) saw a decrease of 74% year-over-year, a result of high investment in tech, investment in user experience, and quick commerce, which has been struggling lately as a result of the intense competition. I've talked about this in more detail in my previous article:

Alibaba is burning cash to buy market share in the on-demand retail space, trying to stand up to the competition from Meituan and others. While revenue in Quick Commerce grew 60% and unit economics are reportedly improving, the immediate impact is a massive hole in the current earnings power. They are essentially reinvesting every dollar of free cash flow back into the business to secure future growth.

Same as the last time around, when adjusted EBITDA saw a major decrease, this time it is down 57% year-over-year to RMB23,397 million (US$3,346 million):

Income from operations was RMB10,645 million (US$1,522 million), a decrease of 74% year-over-year, primarily due to the decrease in adjusted EBITA. Adjusted EBITA, a non-GAAP measurement, decreased 57% year-over-year to RMB23,397 million (US$3,346 million), primarily attributable to the investment in quick commerce, user experiences, and technology, partly offset by the improved operating results supported by continued growth in Cloud business, as well as enhanced operating efficiencies across various businesses.

Net income, directly impacted by the operating margins, came in at RMB15,631 million (US$2,235 million), a decrease of 66% year-over-year. These numbers look more terrifying than they really are, as this is a direct reflection of the AI turnaround, worsened by the quick commerce problems. It doesn't mean the company falls apart, and it will not come out of this cycle; it still has all of the chances to do that, but it definitely means the short term is not going to be pretty. Later, as we talk about the valuation, I'll make an argument that the company, in a heavy turnaround, should be way cheaper for me to want to buy it.

The good news is that the company has a lot of cash, around $80.1 billion, and it gives them an enormous runway to continue this transition. During the first nine months of the fiscal year, they returned $4.8 billion in dividends and $1.1 billion through share repurchases to shareholders, and this should definitely be helpful to make an investment case more attractive for long-term shareholders. At the same time, dividends are currently coming from the cash position this company has, so I think it is not sustainable anymore, and it would be wise to cut the yield. With free cash flow dropping 71% to $1.62 billion this quarter (mostly due to overinvestment in quick commerce and marketing efforts), we most likely will continue seeing large cash burns for longer than the market initially hoped.

Valuation

I know when you open a valuation tab on Seeking Alpha, Alibaba, with a TTM GAAP P/E of 18.3, looks like a value play, especially against the AI U.S. peers, but in my opinion, it is fundamentally wrong to look at it this way.

If you compare it to the Chinese competitors, for example, JD.com (JD) and PDD Holdings, aka Pinduoduo (PDD), it's clear Alibaba is currently trading at a significant premium rather than a discount. With a forward non-GAAP P/E of 21.57, Alibaba is valued at more than double the multiples of JD (9.54) and PDD (9.18). Add to this a transition and major cash burn, and you would see how far down this stock still has to go. BABA is valued not far from Amazon, which is currently a much more stable player, in a way faster-growing market.

The most concerning metric in the valuation is the forward PEG ratio of 2.48, which is the highest among the companies I compared it to. It basically means that you are paying for this turnaround, as though you have a guarantee it will bring desired results, when in reality, nobody can guarantee anything, and for sure not the perfect execution.

Conclusion

Alibaba is in a tough position currently. It tries to fund the AI future, which is a new segment for them, while the legacy e-commerce business struggles to stand the competition. $80.1 billion in cash is a substantial safety net, and this is your guarantee that the company won't fall apart soon, but some of the spending, like dividends, for example, should not be coming out of this money. When free cash flow declines 71% in one quarter, I am even more worried that the management will try to keep up appearances for the shareholders when they really have to start being more careful.

Valuation is enormous for this kind of rough turnaround period. Paying a forward P/E of 21.57 for a Chinese company feels like a mispriced risk when domestic competitors like JD.com and Pinduoduo are trading at single-digit multiples, and they are the ones creating the competition for BABA, which pays a lot of money to fight it. 36% growth in Cloud Intelligence is a step in the right direction, but it is very far from the scale and momentum of global hyperscalers. For these reasons, I am keeping my Hold rating, as the risk-reward doesn't justify a new entry in today's environment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Abeona Therapeutics: Maintaining 'Buy' On ZEVASKYN Launch Potential For RDEB
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Abeona Therapeutics (ABEO), it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Abeona Therapeutics: AAV204 Capsid Could Be Next Growth Prospect Beyond Zevaskyn." With respect to this article, I mentioned that the company was working on developing the novel AIM capsid AAV204 for the treatment of rare eye disorders. As a matter of fact, I spoke about licensing this novel gene therapy capsid technology to use for genetic eye disorders. Well, it entered into a non-exclusive license agreement with Beacon Therapeutics to take this novel AIM capsid AAV204 for the development and commercialization of it to incorporate into gene therapy to treat patients with ophthalmologic indications.

Following 12 months of follow-up work, Beacon exercised its option to obtain a worldwide, non-exclusive license agreement for up to five gene or disease targets. With the ability to expand to another four indications subject to meeting certain conditions laid out. Besides this, the company in its pipeline is working on the development of this novel AIM capsid AAV204 in a gene therapy known as ABO-503. This gene therapy is being developed to target patients with a rare eye disorder known as X-linked retinoschisis [XLRS]. With the first patient receiving ZEVASKYN for recessive dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa [RDEB] in December of 2025, plus the company having a lot of potential with its commercial launch throughout 2026, I believe it is important to maintain a "Buy" rating for this stock.

For the purpose of this article, I'm going to focus on the commercialization efforts of ZEVASKYN. The reason why is because the ABO-503 gene therapy for the treatment of XLRS has been put on hold. The company is not expected to complete its animal efficacy and toxicology studies and cGMP manufacturing of clinical-grade material for this program throughout 2026. The point I'm getting at here is that it is focusing solely on the commercial launch of ZEVASKYN for RDEB for the time being, and the other programs it has, it will continue to work on but will take longer to start human testing for. As I will go over below, the company only needs several patients to attain profitability for its company, which is another reason for maintaining a "Buy" rating.

ZEVASKYN Commercial Launch Efforts Underway and Profitability is Not a Heavy Burden

The company achieved its goal of being able to receive FDA approval of ZEVASKYN [prademagene zamikeracel] for the treatment of wounds in adults and pediatric patients with recessive dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa [RDEB]. As a matter of fact, this isn't just any type of regulatory approval. This therapy is the first and only autologous cell sheet-based gene therapy for these patients. The beauty of this candidate is that it is produced from the patient's very own skin cells. That is, there is a skin biopsy, and the skin itself is sent out to a manufacturing plant. Before going over the latest updates relating to the commercialization efforts of Abeona Therapeutics for ZEVASKYN, I believe it is important to go over what RDEB is and what the possible market opportunity could end up being.

First and foremost, it is important to highlight what DEB, or dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa, is. This type of disorder is such that the skin is fragile and blisters easily. The fragile nature infers that minor scratching or rubbing causes the skin to shear apart. In non-severe cases, it typically only affects the hands, feet, and other extremities of a patient. However, in the severe form, it can lead to blisters all over the body or, even worse, lead to vision loss/scarring. The classic and most severe form of DEB is known as RDEB, or recessive dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa. This form occurs where a patient is born with missing skin all over their body and can also have blisters everywhere. Remember above, where I mentioned that this disorder affects other parts of the body? Well, it does so such that it can affect other mucous-membrane organs. This leads to other symptoms associated with it, such as

  • Blisters in the digestive tract leading to inability to digest and resulting in malnutrition
  • Scarring that leads to joints becoming deformed
  • Eye inflammation leading to vision loss
  • High risk of developing skin cancer known as squamous cell carcinoma

Why does DEB occur? The truth is that it is as a result of a mutation of the COL7A1 gene. The problem is that this gene is important for providing instructions for making subunit proteins to form another protein, known as type VII collagen. This collagen is important for the formation of anchoring fibrils to keep the dermis and epidermis intact [Abeona Therapeutics Corporate Presentation, March 16, 2026, slide #4]. Without the dermis and epidermis layers staying connected, any friction that occurs results in separation of them. The separation between these layers leads to a cascade of the other problems associated with RDEB, which are blisters that then turn into scars.

The thing is that these patients suffer from a host of other problems that they have to deal with. RDEB is troubling because it affects other aspects of a patient's life, such as

  • Emotional burden
  • High suicide rates
  • Physical pain
  • Frequent opioid use

The company successfully completed the phase 3 study, improving two of the main issues associated with RDEB. This was the late-stage trial known as VITAL, with co-primary efficacy endpoints evaluated over a 6-month period. One endpoint was wound healing, and this was measured to achieve a proportion of wound sites that healed by 50% or more from baseline. The second endpoint in place was to improve patient-reported pain. Both of these co-primary efficacy endpoints were achieved in a clinically meaningful and statistically significant manner.

The global epidermolysis bullosa market size is expected to reach $5.78 billion by 2032. This is a very large market opportunity for sure, and gene therapy players are the ones that are looking to improve the quality of life [QoL] for these patients. Although the company estimates that it could end up being a >$4 billion cumulative revenue opportunity. The belief is that there are roughly 750 RDEB patients in the United States that could be treated with ZEVASKYN. However, there is something of importance to keep in mind: given that these patients have wounds all over their bodies, typically one treatment cycle is not enough. The company thinks that each patient could account for about 2 treatment cycles, thus improving the amount of revenue that could be generated. With that being said, it could end up being about 1,500 treatment cycles for the expected pool of patients [Abeona Therapeutics Corporate Presentation March 16, 2026, slide #16]. One important item to highlight is that the company has been able to obtain strong payer momentum. That is, it has been able to gain access to all major commercial payers and all Medicaid programs. It has even gone as far as being able to obtain a J-code for ZEVASKYN. This is crucial for the company to obtain accurate reimbursement for what its therapy is providing.

Only 1 patient has been treated with ZEVASKYN thus far in 2026, but the company notes that it has already collected multiple biopsies, along with many other biopsies already in several different parts of the manufacturing process. In my opinion, it is a bit early in the process in terms of revenue, but the potential is there. That's because it currently is at four Qualified Treatment Centers [QTCs] and expects to onboard an additional five during this year. However, the expectation is that it is going to end 2026 with seven active QTCs. Why would this be necessary? That's because of the demand that has been observed. This latest update showcases that demand doubled from 50 to 100 eligible RDEB patients intending to receive ZEVASKYN. Patient demand is important if Abeona wants to see growth with this gene therapy. What's bullish for the company is that the referrals it is seeing are those of patients far away from QTCs. That is, 70% of such referrals are coming from 300 to 400 miles away [Abeona Therapeutics Corporate Presentation, March 16, 2026, slide #14]. If a patient is willing to travel that far to receive RDEB, then this signifies that patient demand exists for it. Especially the fact that the number of patients wanting to receive it doubled from 50 to 100, as noted above. The most important thing to note is that it only needs to treat at least three patients per month to become profitable in 2026.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Abeona Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments of $191.4 million as of December 31, 2025. The company reported an EPS of $1.01, missing expectations. However, it reported revenues of $5.82 million, which beat by $0.6 million. The truth is that this quarter didn't matter in the grand scheme of things. That's because the company had only launched in Q4 of 2025, as I noted above. Plus, the first patient didn't receive ZEVASKYN for RDEB until December of 2025. The company is ramping up its activation of QTCs so that it can boost its revenues for this gene therapy. I think, thus far, its commercial launch is bullish. Why is that? Well, the demand doubled from 50 to 100 for starters. Secondly, the company is quickly working to activate additional QTCs to treat additional patients. Even though only 1 patient has been given this therapy thus far, it has already collected multiple biopsies and is currently in the manufacturing process.

The thing is that everything noted thus far points to high demand. What remains to be seen is if this demand continues in the next several months. If it does, then I expect this company to not only obtain substantial revenues for ZEVASKYN but also meet its profitability goal as well during this year. Remember, it only needs 3 patients per month to reach this goal. The company burns approximately $93.3 million of cash per year. This is broken down into $26.8 million in R&D expenses, $65 million in SG&A expenses, and then $1.5 million in cost of sales. The thing to note is that I expect the cost of sales number to increase as additional patients are treated with ZEVASKYN for RDEB. The reason for the cash on hand is what I noted in my prior article, which is that the company sold its Priority Review Voucher [PRV]. By selling this, it brought in $155 million in gross proceeds because of it.

The downside is that the company doesn't have a lot of cash on hand at all. What makes me say that? That's because, according to the 10-K SEC Filing, Abeona Therapeutics notes that it believes it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months from the date of the filing [filed on March 17, 2026]. Not only that, but it is going to need additional cash to not only fund its pipeline but also expand its commercialization efforts for ZEVASKYN for the treatment of patients with RDEB. The good news is that it does have a financial tool in place that it could use if it chooses to do so. It has an open market sales agreement with Jefferies to sell up to an aggregate of $75 million worth of its common stock. For the year ending December 31, 2025, it sold 3,510,889 shares of its common stock and generated $17.3 million of net proceeds from it. The point here is that a total aggregate of $51.5 million remains available for sale.

Risks to Business

There are several risks for investors to be aware of before investing in Abeona Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be that of the potential for ZEVASKYN sales. Even though I indicated above that the demand for this gene therapy for RDEB doubled, plus the several QTCs being activated, there is no assurance that this is going to convert to substantial revenues for this company. The hope is that it can continue to build momentum by activating these QTCs in a timely manner and receive reimbursement from all the payors as expected. Another risk is its profitability goal. There is no guarantee that it is going to necessarily be able to meet or manufacture its demand of 3 patients per month to become profitable during 2026.

The second risk to consider would be that of its novel AIM capsid AAV204 as a gene therapy. The company is still in the process of completing its animal efficacy and toxicology studies and cGMP manufacturing of clinical-grade material for the ABO-503 program in 2026. The reason is so that it could be used to treat patients with XLRS. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the safety/pharmacokinetic data from this completed study will be enough to file an IND to begin a phase 1 study. Secondly, the novel AIM capsid AAV204 was developed into a non-exclusive license agreement with Beacon Therapeutics to target several genetic eye disorders. The risk with this is that there is no assurance that this novel AIM capsid is going to be enough to produce positive data for these programs. Nor that Abeona is going to be able to receive its anticipated milestones and royalty payments as a result of this agreement.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of a competitor by the name of Krystal Biotech (KRYS), which has received approval of VYJUVEK [beremagene geperpavec-svdt, or B-VEC] for the treatment of wounds in patients 6 months of age and older with Dystrophic Epidermolysis Bullosa [DEB] with a mutation[s] in the COL7A1 gene. The thing is that this was approved by the FDA back in 2023 but is currently being launched in several other territories like Germany, France, and Japan. In the latest Q4 and full-year 2025 financial results, it was noted to have generated substantial revenues from it. It recorded Q4 2025 VYJUVEK revenues of $107.1 million. Even better, since the commercial launch of this redosable gene therapy topical gel for DEB patients, it has generated $730.3 million since its U.S. launch. Why might there be room for ZEVASKYN? The reason why is because even though VYJUVEK is redosable, it is used for small wounds. Whereas ZEVASKYN is used for larger wounds because it being a cell sheet gene therapy [covering bigger areas]. It is intended to be a one-time gene therapy. However, as I noted above, some patients with wounds all over their bodies might require two treatments instead of one.

Conclusion

Abeona Therapeutics has been able to continue to build upon its FDA approval of ZEVASKYN for the treatment of patients with RDEB. This is evidenced by the fact that the first patient was treated with this cell sheet gene therapy back in December of 2025. The reason why I believe that it has a lot of potential is because of the several positive aspects I laid out above. That is, it was able to, first and foremost, continue to add centers. It has currently been able to activate four QTCs for the time being to offer ZEVASKYN for these patients with RDEB.

However, I believe it is ideal because by the end of 2026, it expects to have up to 7 activated QTCs. Secondly, the demand is definitely there for sure. This was evidenced by the doubling of the patients who wanted to receive this gene therapy. In the prior update, it noted that it had lined up about 50 patients. However, most recently, it noted that the demand grew to 100 patients. The third item is that it has collected several biopsies and has several ongoing in the manufacturing process.

The last item would be the hurdle it needs to become profitable. It only believes that it needs to treat at least three RDEB patients with ZEVASKYN per month to become profitable in 2026. Based on its potential for the commercial launch of this gene therapy for these rare genetic skin disorder patients, plus all the other bullish aspects I described above, I believe that I should maintain a "Buy" rating on this stock.

data.title: Two Harbors: The Merger That Might Not Be
data.description:
Thesis

Two Harbors Investment Corp. (TWO) capital structure owners were ecstatic back in December 2025 when the merger between TWO and UWM Holdings Corporation (UWMC) was announced. The common equity jumped on the day, while the preferred shares and bonds rallied in the subsequent weeks. Things however are turning differently than expected, and in this article we are going to present readers the market signals that indicate the merger might not happen unless terms change. Furthermore we are going to highlight the impact of the binary events on the Series A Preferred Shares (TWO.PR.A), securities which we hold as well.

Initial merger terms

UWMC is a much larger corporation, with a $5.8 billion market value, and a much better credit via its unsecured funding rates. The proposed merger was a fairly standard but conservative one, with only stock for stock consideration:

PONTIAC, Mich. & NEW YORK--(BUSINESS WIRE)--UWM Holdings Corporation (“UWMC”) and Two Harbors Investment Corp. (“TWO”) (NYSE: TWO), today announced that they have entered into a definitive merger agreement pursuant to which UWMC will acquire TWO in an all-stock transaction for $1.3 billion in equity value, based on a fixed exchange ratio of 2.3328x. The transaction has been unanimously approved by the Boards of Directors of both UWMC and TWO. The transaction is expected to close in the second quarter of 2026, subject to approval of TWO’s stockholders and the satisfaction of other closing conditions, including customary regulatory approvals.

Now, as we can see from the above, TWO holders would get a fixed amount of UWMC shares upon the merger completion, thus be fully subject to the equity fluctuations of the UWMC stock. Some merger transactions contain a fixed payment provision to further incentivize holders to vote for the event. Not here.

Basically the selling point for the TWO common shareholders is that their equity stake is set to appreciate in value as part of a larger, more diverse company as UWMC.

As per the announcement, while the boards of directors approved the merger, it was still subject to common shareholders vote. If the TWO common shareholders do not vote in favor, the merger does not happen.

Market signs of trouble

The way things work in the markets is that investors try to anticipate what will happen and vote via the instruments available to them. On the fixed income side, we saw a massive rally in the TWO preferred equity, since UWMC expressed a view that they would retire it upon the merger completion due to the high cost of capital:

TWO.PR.A rallied over 6%, while TWO.PR.C virtually touched par given its first call date in 2026. Now the fixed income securities basically were baking in a successful merger and an upgrade in terms of both credit risk as well as call risk. Quite the rally indeed.

On the common equity side, after such an announcement, the two equities need to trade in tandem if the market believes in the merger. And in the beginning they did:

Notice, though, that since the beginning of March, things have changed. There were slight daily differences, followed by massive divergences in the past days. Why? Because the TWO shareholders seem to have a diverging view on the merger now.

Postponement of merger vote

On March 16, 2026 the vote was supposed to happen for the TWO shareholders:

Two Harbors Investment (TWO) adjourned its special meeting of stockholders on Monday to provide additional time for its shareholders to vote on the proposed acquisition of the mortgage servicing rights-focused REIT by UWM Holdings (UWMC), the company said.

To translate the above in English: We do not have cvorum, so we will postpone the meeting. Because it appears the management team realized they would not get a 'Yes' vote, they postponed the meeting. UWMC is also trying to showcase how fantastic of a company it is, with an impromptu business update:

The company is trying to convince TWO holders that its upside is very large and they should vote in favor of the corporate action. In the chart above they highlight market consensus for their common equity and the upside for TWO holders if they vote in favor of the corporate action.

Furthermore, UWMC management even engaged an external party to help with the strategy around the merger:

It is clear as day that UWMC wants this merger to occur, but the stock price for the company does not help. If the UWMC common shares would have rallied this year, the merger likely would have already been approved. It is not the case, though. The main issue is that the TWO book value is at $11.13 per share as of the latest reporting, while the implied value from the UWMC shares is significantly below that. Why approve a merger where you get shares for a value below book? That is the crux of the issue.

Our view on the forward

Unless we see a market rally from here or an improvement in the UWMC offer, we do not see the merger going through. The market has already voted and said that in the current format the math does not work out. The macro is complicated by the war in Iran, higher rates and inflation fears, all which are pushing rates higher and the UWMC stock price lower.

Impact to TWO.PR.A

We are holders of TWO.PR.A, and we are very sad regarding the current events since the move here is lower. The securities rallied by virtue of being upgraded by the market. UWMC is a much better credit, and the market started pricing TWO.PR.A as such as well. If the merger completely falls through we are going to see a return to higher yields and lower prices:

Before the merger announcement TWO.PR.A was trading with a 23-handle. We are afraid to say it, but we might be heading right back there unless there is an improvement in the merger offer or the market rallies hard.

Latest developments

As we were putting the finishing touches on our article, the TWO management communicated that there is a competing offer to the UWMC one:

Two Harbors Investment (TWO) stock surged 11% in Thursday premarket trading after it received an unsolicited proposal for $10.70 per share in cash, the company said on Thursday. That comes after the mortgage REIT had already agreed to be acquired by mortgage wholesaler UWM Holdings (UWMC) in an all-stock deal that valued each share of TWO at $11.94 on Dec. 16, 2025. At the close of March 18, the UWMC deal valued each share of TWO at ~$8.61.

The common shares rallied hard, while the preferred shares slumped. The reasoning is that there are likely no other highly rated names like UWMC who would take over; thus, while the commons are favored by the cash offer, the preferred shares are not. As a Series A preferred holder, we would like to see the best rated counterpart take over, so that credit spreads tighten. We do not care that much about how the common holders get compensated.

We have to admit, though, that at this juncture, unless UWMC revises their offer, the balance is tilted towards TWO going with the competing offer, which is not advantageous for the preferred holders. All is not lost yet, though, and we are keenly waiting to see if UWMC can send a revised bid given the effort they have put so far into the merger.

Conclusion

TWO.PR.A are preferred shares from Two Harbors (TWO). The securities rallied hard upon the news of the UWMC merger, but have given back some of the gains as the market is beginning to doubt the corporate event. Unless there are changes in the offer or the overall market rallies hard, we do not see the merger going through. This would mean a return to a $23 level price for TWO.PR.A, while an approved merger would see the securities rally back by 4% and have a better volatility profile going forward. We are holding and hoping for an offer improvement by UWMC.

data.title: AT&T: Way Too Late To Be Buying Now
data.description:
AT&T: Keep Calm and Carry On

I don't think AT&T (T) investors have had much to complain about recently, as the company’s fourth-quarter earnings release in late January and the guidance indicate an overly aggressive capital expenditure outlay is clearly a thing of the past, even though those numbers are still expected to stay at a high level. Nevertheless, the telco operator is still delivering pretty solid free cash flow numbers, and ones that suggest the medium-term outlook should not see the company sacrificing margins in favor of pursuing an ill-disciplined capital allocation regime, which should help to alleviate the fears of income investors who could be concerned about the debt leverage encompassing such a strategy. Back in my previous AT&T write-up in October 2025, I made a noteworthy mention about the company's more assured capital spending and relatively stable free cash flow outlook. The market appears to be sending the right signals about T so far, as the stock has remained incredibly resilient.

However, big numbers were thrown about recently, as AT&T is reportedly looking to “spend $250B building out its network over the next five years.” That caught my eye because it was only in January when T provided what I thought to be a reasonably comfortable range in terms of capital expenditure for investors to digest and take away from, since those are annualized figures to start with. In essence, management promulgated an annual CapEx outlay of $23.5B at the midpoint while also enunciating steadily increasing free cash flow numbers of $18B+ for this year before reaching more than 21 billion dollars by FY2028.

So, why is AT&T looking to shake the boat so soon after posting its full-year guidance just less than two months ago? Fortuitously, those figures included long-term operating costs, not just CapEx. Importantly, no changes were made to the outlook for CapEx guidance, which would have necessitated significant changes to analyst estimates for the next five years. Income investors who have invested consistently with AT&T over the past few years shouldn't be alarmed by the headline figure, as the key CapEx metrics are, after all, not materially impacted.

AT&T's Broadband Satellite Forays Offers Optionality but Not Near-Term Drivers

Nevertheless, I could see that AT&T is leaning in further with the AST SpaceMobile collaboration (ASTS) to “extend coverage into remote areas,” and making broadband satellite connectivity one of its major growth pillars through the rest of the decade. I thought the timing was especially apt, since the much-anticipated SpaceX’s IPO (SPACE) could make such offerings more mainstream while allowing the Elon Musk-led company a lot more leverage and financial capabilities to proliferate its leadership and expand its market exposure into new growth areas while competing with AT&T. These are exciting milestones that could elevate investment sentiments, right? But in my opinion, I think it's still too nascent for us to ascertain the success of the partnership. After all, the company’s current strategy is still based on its convergence framework. And that’s largely predicated upon the success of offering “historically separate networks like 5G, fiber optic broadband, and Wi-Fi on one platform.”

Therefore, I believe the primary gameplay for AT&T in the near term will still focus on this framework while building out its broadband satellite connectivity business to be a more significant contributor subsequently. It’s notable that “42% of households that have AT&T fiber now also choose AT&T for wireless,” giving the company a clear runway to increase its penetration rate over time. And, AT&T has also emphasized the importance of this thesis during a recent conference, as they explained:

Just to level-set, we ended last year with 32 million fiber passings. With the closing of the Lumen transaction, we are now over 36 million. We expect to end this year at 40 million fiber passings. So incrementally, for this year, we are adding over 8 million fiber locations. With each of those fiber locations comes a convergence opportunity. You should expect us to drive incremental share take in -- where we are expanding our footprint, plus the build that -- the existing build, which there's still opportunity to drive further penetration. And that's going to drive not only fiber volumes, but that will drive also wireless. I mentioned earlier, our penetration in the Lumen footprint, of convergence, the convergence in the Lumen footprint is significantly less than our own. And so you should expect us to really focus on driving incremental convergence in that footprint. So those 2 things should allow us to really drive share take in fiber and mobility. - AT&T Inc. Presents at Deutsche Bank 34th Annual Media, Internet & Telecom Conference Transcript

I thought these were crucial guideposts for investors to contemplate because they give us confidence that the convergence narrative is working fine, even as the company seeks a new, more exciting growth vector outside its current core capabilities.

Given that AT&T’s forward earnings multiple of 11.9x is not quite configured for growth (where's the implied premium?), I do not believe it will feature prominently among the shareholder class demanding clear visibility into the satellite connectivity business for now. That will definitely remain a work in progress with future optionality, and I believe the market will consider it just fine, as AT&T focuses on its core business while working to return to its target leverage ratio of 2.5x and delivering on its promises of hitting >$21B of FCF by 2028, less than two FYs from now.

AT&T Must Maintain Capital Allocation Discipline

Wall Street is largely confident that AT&T can deliver on its outlook, slotting in the FCF outlook for 2028 at just under $21B. Given the visibility and relatively solid metrics that T has posted recently, while also having a less onerous regulatory climate, I feel confident that the company should be able to sustain its incremental share gains through its recent integration with the Lumen transaction, expanding its footprint.

Notably, we could expect some near-term pressure on expecting a materially higher ARPU as part of the convergence story, with imputed discounts to encourage signups, but I don't expect the overall margins to be markedly affected. While the opportunity for longer-term accretion could be a welcome growth lever, I think the cautious optimism indicated by management should help to provide some flexibility for the company to grow into its expanded opportunity with the Lumen deal, as they emphasized that they “wouldn't expect a meaningful contribution from ARPU for both wireless and fiber.”

Buy Or Sell T Stock?

So after we have sorted out its fundamentals, its near-term growth drivers, and also the optionality emanating from its partnership with AST SpaceMobile, I believe the optimism has already been somewhat captured in T’s price action. As we can glean from the above chart, T buyers have been unable to muster a decisive breakthrough above the $30 zone, making it a significant resistance level that could deny further upside unless we see a more profound upgrade to its medium-term outlook. Failing which, I believe the stock should continue to hover around or below these levels, even though the bearish downtrend is well and truly over.

T’s relatively cheap multiples should still help keep income investors on board, although the forward yield of 4% is arguably too low if I were to consider that the 10Y is still printing at 4.3%, which offers a much more competitive rate.

Hence, I think the market is somewhat quite nonchalant about AT&T's buying opportunity at the current level, but I could see a potential entry point for opportunistic investors to consider. If a further fallout in the equity markets were to allow the stock to decline back to just above the $20 level, then I think another opportunity to get more engaged might just materialize. Until then, I think we should just allow this market action to play out and remain on the sidelines for now.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

data.title: How Much Does Management Matter For A Publicly Traded Company?
data.description:
When we reviewed the carnage among business development companies, or BDCs, when recapping February 2026's dividend decreases, its concentration within this sub-sector of the financial services sector of the U.S. economy really stood out.

BDCs make their money by loaning money they either raise from investors or borrow themselves to small- and medium-sized enterprises that can't raise money by going public and selling stock and also financially distressed businesses. The business models of most established BDCs involve borrowing money, then loaning it back out at higher interest rates, where they pocket the difference.

That makes the profit margins of BDCs vulnerable to rate cuts. Because their loans are tied to the Federal Funds Rate, when the Fed cuts that rate, it negatively impacts BDC profits. In the last three years, BDCs have gone from a rising or high interest rate environment (March 2023 through August 2025) to a falling rate environment (September 2024 through December 2025).

The performance of the VanEck BDC Income Exchange Traded Fund (BIZD), which includes over 30 BDCs in its market cap-weighted index, gives a good sense of how BDCs performed in these different environments. The following chart shows BDCs rising or flat in the rising rate environment, but then either stalling or falling as the Fed shifted gears into its rate-cutting mode.

But that's not the whole story. During the rate-cutting period, which initiated the pressure on BDC profits, BDCs have had to cope with the DeepSeek AI shock, peaking just ahead of that event on 19 February 2025. Then they faced the Liberation Day global tariffs shock event of 2 April 2025, plunging with the rest of the market before going on to recover. That lasted until August 2025, when the return of rate cuts initiated a new downtrend that was followed in January 2026 with a new AI shock event that undermined the business prospects of the Software-As-A-Service (SaaS) firms. Many of which were getting their funding to grow from BDCs.

With AI technologies seemingly set to destroy any potential profitability these firms had, many BDCs were suddenly faced with having to write down large portions of their portfolios. But not all BDCs are in that boat.

When we looked at the stock performance of individual BDCs, we found a clear characteristic that divided them. That characteristic is their governance, and what quickly became evident was that internally managed BDCs were generally outperforming BDCs whose investments are managed by external parties.

To illustrate that difference, we randomly selected six externally managed BDCs to compare their performance against an equal number of internally managed BDCs over the last three years. Here is a list of the BDCs in our performance sample:

Externally Managed BDCs

  • Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC)
  • Fidus Investment (FDUS)
  • Kayne Anderson BDC (KBDC)
  • Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL)
  • Nuveen Churchill Direct Lending (NCDL)
  • Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX)

Internally Managed BDCs

  • Capital Southwest (CSWC)
  • Gladstone Capital (GLAD)
  • Main Street Capital (MAIN)
  • Phenixfin (PFX)
  • Rand Capital (RAND)
  • Trinity Capital (TRIN)

Let's get to the results. The following chart visualizes the relative performance of the stocks of the two kinds of BDCs:

We've shown the 3-year returns for the benchmarks of the S&P 500 (SPX) at 72.01% and BIZD at -10.79% to show how they compare against the range of the two categories. The externally managed BDCs range from a high of +2.79% to a low of -29.22%, with four of the six BDCs having a negative return.

By contrast, the internally managed BDCs range from a high of +44.22% to a low of -16.96%, with two of the six BDCs having a negative return.

But it's not just recent market events driving that outcome. In the next two charts, we show how the sample of internally managed and externally managed BDCs compares with the performance of the S&P 500 over the last three years. The first chart tracks the internally managed BDCs:

The next chart follows the externally managed BDCs over the same period.

We find the internally managed BDCs have sustained better performance than the externally managed BDCs over all portions of this three-year period, which can be seen in their relative performance being closer to that of the benchmark S&P 500 index. That better performance occurred both in a period in which rising interest rates provided BDCs with a tailwind and the current period in which falling interest rates are providing fierce headwinds against the BDCs.

When we started this exercise, we thought we'd mainly be discussing the role of how changing interest rates have affected the performance of the BDC sub-sector of the financial services industry, leading so many of these firms to cut their dividends in recent months. We didn't expect to run into a more interesting question: how much does management matter in a publicly traded company? In the case of BDCs, whether the people managing their lending business work directly for the firm or are employed outside of it would appear to have a significant impact affecting the returns of the shareholders who own the companies.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Buy The Dip: Best Stocks With Forward EPS Growth Above 40%
data.description:
Strong Earnings Outlook Bolsters Market

The Fed’s decision to keep interest rates unchanged, hotter-than-expected inflation data, and surging oil prices triggered a broad market selloff that spared no sector. However, prior to this pullback, markets had weathered volatility tied to the Iran war and concerns over stretched valuations.

The key has been robust earnings forecasts, which have improved since the outbreak of hostilities. S&P 500 earnings are expected to grow by 15.3% in CY 2026 and 11.6% in Q1, which would mark a sixth-straight quarter of double-digit YoY growth. Q1 growth estimates are being led by AI-fueled technology sector stocks (+42% YoY), followed by materials and financials, according to FactSet data.

Recent market volatility has pulled down a broad range of stocks, including companies with attractive fundamentals and solid earnings growth outlooks. In several cases, the decline appears driven more by external factors – such as shifting monetary policy expectations, geopolitical developments, and macroeconomic data – than by any deterioration in underlying business performance.

The opportunity lies in identifying high-quality companies where fundamentals remain intact, but valuations have compressed alongside broader market volatility. In this environment, a disciplined, data-driven approach can help isolate stocks with strong earnings growth, profitability, and valuation support.

How I Chose Top Stocks With Strong Forward EPS Growth

Using Seeking Alpha’s Stock Screener, I filtered for Strong Buys with high forward EPS growth rates and strong Growth and EPS Revision factors grades. I then narrowed the list to stocks that experienced recent pullbacks, including double-digit declines in the past 30 days that also possessed attractive valuations based on PEG ratios. Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings are generated by a proprietary model that analyzes more than 100 metrics for each stock relative to sector peers and grades them across five factors: Growth, Value, Profitability, Earnings Revisions, and Momentum.

1. Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (CRDO)

  • Market Capitalization: $18.76B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Semiconductor

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 3/19/2026): 18 out of 525

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 3/19/2026): 4 out of 69

Recently added to the PRO Quant Portfolio and a member of Alpha Picks, Credo is a developer of connectivity solutions for AI infrastructure and among the fastest growing microchip stocks in the past year, outpacing large-cap peers in earnings and revenue growth.

Shares fell this week when Nvidia (NVDA) said it would use copper and optical cables in parallel as it scales up next-generation AI systems, even though Credo supplies connectivity used in both technologies. In fact, BNP Paribas analyst Karl Ackerman sees Nvidia's dual-pronged approach as "bullish" for the company.

CRDO vs. S&P Semiconductor Select Index (XSD): 1Y Price Return

Despite another earnings beat and guidance lift, Credo’s stock pulled back after falling just short of high gross margin expectations. Credo’s revenue grew by over 200% to $407M in Q3 2026 and EPS of $1.07 beat by $0.13, driven by continued strength in active electrical cables (AECs) and integrated circuits (ICs).

Looking ahead, Credo expects new acquisitions and product upgrades to expand its addressable market across the full spectrum of data center connectivity – from optical networking to the chip level – to deliver energy-efficient AI clusters, according to CEO Bill Brennan.

“With continued growth in AECs and ICs and the announcement of three new multi-billion dollar TAM expansions through ZeroFlap optics, ALCs, and OmniConnect, we remain confident in our ability to innovate and grow in the expanding AI infrastructure landscape,” Brennan said in a press release.

With 14 upward earnings revisions to no down revisions in the past 90 days, analysts are increasingly bullish on Credo’s outlook. EPS is projected to grow by 372% in FY 2026 and revenue by over 200% to $1.33B. Expanding margins have been key drivers of Credo’s exceptional Profitability Grade, along with robust return on equity and capital.
